draft.introduction: The Alagaḍḍūpama Sutta warns against grasping the dhamma wrongly. Like catching a snake by the tail, misunderstanding the teaching brings harm rather than liberation.

translation.text: A monk named Ariṭṭha had formed the pernicious view that sensual pleasures are no obstruction. The Blessed One said: “Misguided man, to whom have I ever taught the dhamma in that way?”

Monks, I shall show you how the dhamma is similar to a raft, being for the purpose of crossing over, not for the purpose of holding onto. When you see its meaning, you should let even good teachings go, how much more so bad teachings.
translation.original_text: kullaūpamaṃ vo bhikkhave desessami dhammaṃ nittharanathāya no gahanatthāya

translator_notes.note: The raft simile is one of Buddhism’s most cited metaphors. If the dharma itself must be abandoned upon reaching the far shore, then no teaching—not even the Buddha’s—has ultimate, independent validity. This radical pragmatism distinguishes Buddhism from traditions that treat their scriptures as eternally binding.
